parts.text:
**My Process: Navigating Trauma through Narrative**

Okay, so I’ve got this character, Dani Reyes. 21, junior, Environmental Science, always biting her cheek when she’s thinking, hair tie always on her wrist. This Phi Delta Sigma party, October 11, 2031. She goes upstairs with this guy. It goes sideways. I need to write *through* it, not around it. Visceral, immediate, Dani's voice. I gotta get that careful, observant nature in there, the detachment that slowly cracks. It's about psychological realism, her specific coping mechanisms.

First, I'm setting the stage: the party scene. The bass, the humidity, her hair. Priya is probably involved, maybe even pushed her to go. I'm focusing on "the guy"—the one she thought she had read correctly. This feeling of misplaced confidence in her ability to read people will be key. This impulsive decision to go upstairs. This initial assessment—is he safe? I'm thinking I'll avoid naming him, just 'the guy in the Henley' or something like that.

Then, the transition. "Let's go somewhere quieter." The impulsive decision again. The sticky stairs. The smell of his room. The intimacy starts, and then it's too much. The turning point: "Wait." "No, hold on." I need to show the mechanics of it—the physical weight, her Dad's hands pushing, the hair tie. She bites inside her cheek and tastes blood. That feeling of being trapped. The detachment—looking at the ceiling, analyzing the water stain, noticing the mundane details, like the environmental science brain.

The aftermath: Getting out. The cold air. Escape. The disconnect. Back to her room. Biting her cheek. Priya is out. This is all the more traumatic for it because it's not something you can just run to Priya and unload on. The familiar seeking the normal—oatmeal and pad see ew thoughts. The Notes app. Fragmented thoughts. The raw entry.

I need to connect it to her core traits. The need for routine. How this breaks that routine. The feeling of being misread, or reading someone else wrong. That connection to Kayla—being made a joke, being powerless. This is the same, but infinitely worse. And then, finally, that raw Notes app entry ending with `[DONE]`. It’s about more than the event; it's about Dani's internal world. Her observations, her fears, her attempts to process this.

The basement of Phi Delta Sigma feels like the inside of a lung. It’s hot, damp, and expanding with the bass from the speakers. It’s so humid down here that I can feel my hair curling against the back of my neck, getting heavy and wavy. I’ve been chewing on the inside of my left cheek for twenty minutes, tasting the faint, metallic tang of copper. 

Priya dragged me here. She’s currently across the room, being glowing and warm with a guy from her lab, draining her social battery in a way she won’t even realize until tomorrow. I was going to leave. I had the impulsive thought to just walk out, ghost the party, go back to our room, and eat peanut butter out of the jar. 

But then he started talking to me. 

He’s in my stats class, the one I have to take because environmental science is really just numbers and begging people for money. I think his name is Chris. Or maybe Colin. He asked about my uncle’s farm in Ohio, remembered me mentioning it in some icebreaker on the first day. I thought I was reading him right. I usually think I’m good at reading people. I rely on that sixty percent success rate like it’s a shield. 

"It’s too loud to hear anything down here," he says. He leans in close so I can hear him. He smells like Tide pods and cheap beer. "Want to go upstairs?"

An impulsive decision. But it’s loud, and I’m tired of yelling over a remix of a song I don’t even like. And I am careful. I scan his face. He’s smiling. It’s a soft, slightly goofy smile. 

"Sure," I say. 

We walk up the stairs. My shoes stick to the treads. The second floor is quieter, the bass muffled by the floorboards. We go into a bedroom at the end of the hall. It’s a standard frat room. Posters. A desk covered in Solo cups. He shuts the door. 

He kisses me. It’s fine. It’s normal. I kiss him back. My hands are on his chest. 

Then it stops being normal. 

He pushes me back toward the bed. I hit the mattress, and suddenly his entire body weight is on top of me. It’s abrupt. The air leaves my lungs. His hands are under my shirt, pulling at the fabric, his mouth hot and wet against my neck. 

"Wait," I say. I try to laugh a little, to make it a joke, to de-escalate. "Hold on. Too fast."

He doesn’t stop. He shifts, pinning my leg with his knee. 

"Chris, wait," I say, louder this time. My voice sounds thin in the room. 

"You're fine," he murmurs into my shoulder. "You're good."

"No," I say. "No, I don't want to. Get up."

I use my hands—my dad’s hands, the wide palms, the short fingers—to push against his shoulders. I push hard. I expect him to move. Physics says he should move. But he’s so much bigger than me, and he just drops his dead weight, smothering the leverage I have. 

He doesn’t stop. He pulls at my jeans. 

"Stop," I say. I’m not yelling. Why aren't I yelling? I’m frozen between disbelief and a sudden, sharp spike of terror. *He heard me. I know he heard me.* 

He grabs my left wrist and pushes it down into the mattress. The black hair tie I always keep there digs violently into the skin of my wrist bone. It hurts. The sharp, pinching pain of the elastic is so distinct, so hyper-specific, that my brain latches onto it. 

He doesn't stop. 

I stop fighting. I don’t know why. My brain just switches off the circuit breaker. I look up at the ceiling. There’s a water stain in the corner of the drywall. It looks like the map of a lake. I stare at it. I think about the water table in rural Ohio, how when something is poisoned underground, the surface looks completely fine until it doesn't. You can't tell just by looking. I bite the inside of my cheek. I bite it so hard my jaw aches. 

It doesn't take very long. When he's done, he rolls off me, breathing heavily. He sits on the edge of the bed and reaches for his phone. 

"You good?" he asks, not looking at me. 

I don't say anything. I sit up. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely manage the button on my jeans. I pull my shirt down. I stand up. I walk to the door, turn the handle, and leave. 

I walk down the stairs. The party is exactly the same as I left it. The bass. The sweat. Priya is laughing somewhere. Nobody looks at me. Nobody knows. 

I walk out the front door and into the October air. It’s freezing. I start walking fast. Then I’m running. I have no playlist for this. No boygenius, no black midi, no Paramore. Just the ragged, wet sound of my own breathing, my sneakers slapping the concrete all the way back to the dorm. 

The room is empty. Priya isn't back. 

I sit on my bed. I haven't turned the lights on. The streetlamp outside casts a long, yellow rectangle across the floor. I sit there for twenty minutes. I feel entirely hollowed out. I feel like fourteen-year-old Dani, looking at Kayla after she told the boy I liked him, realizing I had been made into a joke without my permission. My agency, stripped. My voice, ignored. Turned into an object. A punchline. Except this isn't a joke. 

I pull my knees to my chest. I need to text Mel. I need to send her a voice memo. I open our thread. I stare at the little microphone icon. 

*I can't.* 

I close her text thread. I open the Notes app. A new note. I stare at the blinking cursor in the dark. 

***

**October 11, 2031**

I said no. 
I know I said it. 
I said no and he heard me and he just decided it didn't matter. 

I am sixty percent good at reading people. I was wrong. I was so wrong. 

My wrist hurts. The hair tie left a red indent in my skin. It looks like a burn. 

I don't know what to do. I don't know how to eat oatmeal tomorrow. I don't know how I'm going to order pad see ew on Tuesday. I don't know how I'm going to sit in stats on Wednesday and look at the back of his head. 

He asked if I was good. He actually asked me that. 

I pushed him. I tried. Dad's hands. They weren't big enough. 

It's like the water table. Something is fundamentally contaminated right now, under the soil, deep down where nobody can see it. And I'm just supposed to walk around on the grass and pretend it's fine. 

I am so cold.

[DONE]
